coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on a truncated specific aptamer by MGO-SELEX screening for streptomycin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wanqi
Sun, Zhicong
Tian, Yuhang
Mou, Yaoting
Guo, Yemin
Sun, Xia
Li, Falan
coredata.dc:description: Streptomycin (STR) is an antibiotic fungicide that is easily enriched in the environment and threatens human health. In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor with silver nanoclusters-skimmed milk powder@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8) fluorescent material as the response signal and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functionalized with aptamer as the reference signal was prepared. Magnetic graphene oxide selection technology was used to screen high-affinity STR-specific aptamer. The truncation strategy for removing non-binding site bases was based on molecular simulation docking, and it was found that the stem-loop structure formed by the truncated aptamer 52 (Apt52) was used for STR-specific recognition and had a higher STR affinity (K d = 14.52 nM) compared to the initial 79-nucleotide aptamer. AgNCs was then covered in a protein coating and encapsulated in ZIF-8 to obtain AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 with enhanced red fluorescence intensity. The added Cu2+ rapidly penetrated into ZIF-8 to induce red fluorescence quenching, while the CQDs modified by Apt52 maintained green fluorescence. Hence, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor with Apt52 as the recognizing element can detect STR within 90 s with a detection limit of 0.98 nM. The ratiometric probe proposed has considerable potential for STR residue detection in milk.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptamer
MGO-SELEX
Streptomycin
Fluorescence
Ratiometric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot fluorescent probe
Dual fluorescent signaling proportional aptamer sensor for STR detection
Detection of STR in real milk samples
STR detection by proportional fluorescence method
Specificity and stability of aptamer sensor
Ability of the proportional fluorescence sensor to detect STR in milk

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023T160393, 2022M721977), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32001781), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2020QC249), Shandong Province Science and Technology Small and Medium Enterprises Innovation Ability Enhancement Project (2022TSGC1188), Shandong Provincial Youth Innovation Team Development Plan of Colleges and Universities (2022KJ226).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of MGO-SELEX screening for STR aptamer.
(A) K d value saturation curves of five aptamers after truncation. (B) CD spectrum of Apt52 in the presence and absence of targets. Error bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 3).

(A) TEM image of AgNCs and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. (B) SEM image of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. (C) The EDS spectra of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 composing of C, N, O, Zn, and Ag elements.(D) FT-IR spectrum of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8, ZIF-8, and AgNCs. (E) XRD patterns of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8. (F) Fluorescence intensities among AgNCs, SMP-coated AgNCs, and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8.
Schematic of an aptasensor for detection of STR.

(A) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of AgNCs synthesized at different pH. (B) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of AgNCs mediated by different proteins. (C) Effect of SMP concentration on the encapsulation effect of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. (D) Exploration of the volume ratio of detection probe and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. (E) Fluorescence intensity quenching of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 by different concentrations of Cu2+ in the presence of 1 nM and 100 nM STR. (F) Study of relative fluorescence intensity variation with exposure time in the presence of Cu2+ (100 ng/mL). Error bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 3).

(A) Fluorescence change curves and (B) calibrated detection curves for the quenching efficiency of Cu2+ in the presence of different concentrations of STR (1–500 nM). Error bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on a truncated specific aptamer by MGO-SELEX screening for streptomycin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo 255049, Shandong Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Vegetable Safety and Quality Traceability, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo 255049, Shandong Province, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology, No. 266 Xincun Xilu, Zibo 255049, Shandong Province, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Streptomycin (STR) is an antibiotic fungicide that is easily enriched in the environment and threatens human health. In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor with silver nanoclusters-skimmed milk powder@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8) fluorescent material as the response signal and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functionalized with aptamer as the reference signal was prepared. Magnetic graphene oxide selection technology was used to screen high-affinity STR-specific aptamer. The truncation strategy for removing non-binding site bases was based on molecular simulation docking, and it was found that the stem-loop structure formed by the truncated aptamer 52 (Apt52) was used for STR-specific recognition and had a higher STR affinity (K d = 14.52 nM) compared to the initial 79-nucleotide aptamer. AgNCs was then covered in a protein coating and encapsulated in ZIF-8 to obtain AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 with enhanced red fluorescence intensity. The added Cu2+ rapidly penetrated into ZIF-8 to induce red fluorescence quenching, while the CQDs modified by Apt52 maintained green fluorescence. Hence, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor with Apt52 as the recognizing element can detect STR within 90 s with a detection limit of 0.98 nM. The ratiometric probe proposed has considerable potential for STR residue detection in milk.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Streptomycin (STR) is an aminoglycoside antibiotic, which is often used in animal husbandry to suppress or treat diseases caused by microbial infections due to its cheap and effective advantages [1,2]. However, the misuse of STR may cause residues in food of animal origin, or even lead to bacterial mutation and antibiotic resistance, which can spread to humans through the food chain, water environment and soil microorganisms, causing ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity [3]. Antibiotic residues in raw milk will inhibit the propagation of fermentation strains, which decreases the quality of dairy products. In order to ensure human health and the development of the dairy industry, both the European Commission and the Chinese Ministry of Agriculture have set the maximum residue level of STR in milk at 200 μg/kg [4]. Thus, there is a need to establish a rapid, sensitive, and accurate method for the detection of STR residues.

Common STR detection methods include instrumental analysis [5], microbial detection [6], or enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [7]. However, these methods require expensive equipment, have slow detection speeds, and depend on professional technicians. Moreover, many innovative aptasensors for detecting STR have been reported in recent years, which use an aptamer as the recognition element to specifically bind to a target, which is converted into signals that can be analyzed and detected through a signal converter. Fluorescence signal is highly compatible with the aptamer. At present, there are abundant stable fluorophores that can be used to modify aptamers. In addition, fluorophores with different emission wavelengths enable multi-targets detection and reduce background signal interference [8]. Fluorescence methods are characterized by high sensitivity, specificity and accuracy. The construction of aptasensors using fluorescence signal also has the advantage of low cost. Due to these fantastic characteristics of fluorescence technology, significant scientific researches in the past few years have been directed at designing fluorescent aptasensors based on the change of the fluorescence signal after interactions between fluorescent probe and target [9–11].

Aptamers, also known as "chemical antibody", usually refer to oligonucleotide fragments with high affinity to target substances obtained from random DNA libraries by using systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) [12]. Aptamers have significant advantages such as small size, easy synthesis in vitro, low immunogenicity and high stability, allowing the wide use of aptamers in applications of food safety analysis, environmental monitoring and drug target screening [13–16]. With the continuous innovation and development of aptamer technologies, many new SELEX methods have recently been developed to simplify the aptamer screening procedure for a variety of targets [17], such as capillary electrophoresis-SELEX [18,19], cell-SELEX [20], magnetic beads-SELEX [21]. Although a range of aptamers has been obtained through these novel screening methods above, it is still challenging for small-molecule targets [22,23]. Screening with solid carriers is unfeasible due to the lack of functional groups for immobilizing small molecules [24]. Magnetic graphene oxide (MGO) is an inorganic composite material composed of graphene oxide and magnetic materials with good hydrophilicity, excellent biocompatibility, huge specific surface area and magnetic separation properties. Therefore, MGO-SELEX can also adsorb and immobilize free ssDNA or RNA for free-state target aptamer screening through hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking interactions [25,26]. Not only does MGO-SELEX solve the problem of low molecular weight of the target, but can improve the screening efficiency through magnetic enrichment and separation processes. This is applicable to the screening of small molecule target aptamers [27,28].

Metal nanoclusters are promising fluorescent probes used in biochemical analysis, environmental detection, and medical imaging due to their low toxicity, easy synthesis, and good biocompatibility [29,30]. However, some metal nanoclusters suffer from a low quantum yield or poor stability [31]. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a new type of porous crystalline material that combines the high stability of inorganic molecular sieves with the organic functionality and high porosity of metal organic frameworks (MOFs). It has the significant advantages of high tunable capacity, convenient synthesis, large surface area, and good biocompatibility [32]. Many reports have realized aggregation-induced fluorescence emission enhancement by embedding metallic nanomaterials into the pores of MOFs and found that the quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime were also enhanced [33–35]. Analogous nanocomposites can be used to construct fluorescent sensors that retain the unique adsorption properties of MOFs, as well as the fluorescence properties of metal nanoclusters.

Conventional single-signal mode sensors rely only on having a corresponding single signal output for a given input signal. However, in actual sensing systems, the signal responsive to the target may be inaccurate and unreliable if the material inside the sensor changes or if the external environmental conditions affect the sensing and the background signal interferes. In contrast, ratiometric dual-signal sensors have a stabilizing reference factor with built-in calibration to prevent the output signal from being affected by background interference, resulting in more accurate detection results [36,37]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) provide stable fluorescence signal due to their uniform size, stability, and superior fluorescence properties. Therefore, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor can be constructed using immobilized CQDs as the fluorescence signal and silver nanoclusters-skimmed milk powder@ZIF-8 (AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8).

In this study, a novel and good aptamer selection method, MGO-SELEX, was used to screen STR-specific aptamers. In order to obtain a lower cost and higher performance aptamer, the long stem portion that binds to STR was retained by rational truncation based on secondary structure and molecular modeling of the docking site. The binding affinities of the truncated and full-length aptamers for STR were compared using fluorescent labeling. The affinity of the aptamer screened by this method was enhanced compared to the previous STR aptamers. SMP protein wrapped and ZIF-8 encapsulated AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 has a strong fluorescence signal, high stability and high sensitivity. On the one hand, ZIF-8 can modulate AgNCs within the rigid framework of MOFs to spatially separate AgNCs in a certain direction and improve the luminescence performance and stability of AgNCs. On the other hand, the porous ZIF-8 shell can absorb the accumulated quencher Cu2+ to a large extent thereby accelerating the Cu2+-AgNCs interaction and ensuring the red fluorescence signal of the nanocomposites responds to Cu2+ faster. In summary, a ratiometric fluorescence detection of STR was established using AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 composite as the response signal, combined with CQDs as the reference signal, and the high-affinity truncation aptamer 52 (Apt52), and compared with other STR detection techniques. This study not only provides a new idea to improve the binding affinity of the aptamer, but also provides a new detection platform for STR detection.

The initial SELEX library and the forward and reverse primers for polymerase chain reaction (PCR) were custom-synthesized and purified by high-performance liquid chromatography by Shanghai Sangong Bioengineering Technical Services Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All of the above sequences are shown in Table S1. DNA dye GelRed, Taq PCR Mix (2 ×), and magnetic beads modified by streptavidin were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). DNA product purification/gel recycling kit was purchased from Tiangen Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). 6 × loading buffer was acquired from Takara Physical Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). 2-Methylimidazole, zinc acetate, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and antibiotics including STR, penicillin G (PCN), chloramphenicol (CAP), cefapirin (CEP), and tetracycline (TET) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). MGO powder was purchased from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). DNA marker and agarose were obtained from Takara Biomedical Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and ovalbumin (OVA) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). CQDs were purchased from Xi'an RuiXi Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanxi, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water by water purification system (PALL, USA, 18.2 MΩ·cm at 25 ℃) was utilized for preparing all configured aqueous solution.

NanoDrop 2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Co., USA) was used to quantitatively determine the DNA concentration. PCR amplification was performed using a TC-96 PCR amplifier (Hangzhou Bioer Technology Co., Ltd. Hangzhou, China). A fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan) was used for aptamer affinity determination and sensor fluorescence intensity measurements. Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy was performed on a J-1500 polarization spectrometer (Jasco Co., Ltd. Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were obtained by FEI Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN at 200 kV (FEI, USA). The scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were obtained by the Quanta 250FEG (USA). The Fourier-transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum were obtained using Nicolet iS5 FTIR (ThermoI, USA). The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns were performed from SmartLab (Rigaku Co., Ltd. Japan).

During the first round of screening, 1 μL of denatured random oligonucleotide library (100 μM) was mixed with 199 μL of binding buffer solution (20 mM Tris-HCl, 100 mM NaCl, 2 mM MgCl2, 5 mM KCl, 1 mM CaCl2, and 0.02% Tween 20, pH 7.6) with gentle oscillation. The target was added and incubated at 25 °C protected from light for 1 h. Subsequently, 75 μL of 2 mg/mL MGO solution was added and incubated for 1.5 h at room temperature away from light. With the addition of MGO, free ssDNA was adsorbed onto the MGO surface through π-π stacking interactions. Next, the supernatant was collected after centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and concentrated using a DNA purification kit.

The 25 μL PCR mixture contained 1 μL of purified supernatant, 1 μL of forward primer, 1 μL of biotinylated reverse primer, 12.5 μL of Taq PCR premix, and 9.5 μL of sterilized water. The PCR amplification process has been reported by Yue et al. [38], the details are as follows: the configured mixture was pre-denatured at 95 °C for 5 min, then cycled 30 times denaturation processes at 95 °C for 30 s, annealed at 55 °C for 30 s, and extended at 75 °C for 15 s with these three sections. Finally, it was extended at 72 °C for 10 min and naturally cooled to 4 °C.

After boiling 20 mL of 1 × TAE electrophoresis solution containing 0.6 g agarose, 2 μL of nucleic acid dye was dropped to formulate a 3% agarose gel. 1 μL of 6 × loading buffer and 5 μL of PCR amplification products were mixed and added to the gel wells. The mix PCR products were identified by electrophoresis and gel imager. Biotinylated dsDNA was isolated by streptavidin-modified magnetic beads, and non-biotinylated ssDNA was collected using sodium hydroxide elution as the initial library for the next round of SELEX. Screening was terminated when the gel electrophoresis bands were correctly and clearly positioned and the recoveries calculated from the measured nucleic acid concentrations have stabilized.

The PCR amplification products obtained from the final round of screening were sent to Bioengineering Biotechnology (Shanghai, China) for high-throughput sequencing. Thirty sequences were analyzed for their homology and secondary structure and were classified into different families. Representative sequences from each family were selected to label fluorescence for subsequent affinity and specificity analysis. The labeled fluorescent aptamer was first denatured at 95 °C for 10 min, cooled at 4 °C for 10 min, and left at room temperature for 10 min. Different concentrations (5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, and 160 nM) of aptamer were incubated with 1 μM STR for 1 h at room temperature. Then, 2 mg/mL MGO was added to adsorb the aptamer for 1.5 h. Binding buffer was used to replace the target standard as a blank control. Next, the supernatant was collected after magnetic separation, and the fluorescence data obtained at different concentrations of supernatants were recorded. The corresponding affinity curves based on the difference in fluorescence intensity between the experimental groups and the blank groups were fitted. According to dissociation constants (K d) value and molecular simulation docking and binding site analysis, the fluorescence intensity of the truncated optimized aptamer was re-measured using the same steps, and the truncated affinity curve was obtained.

The AgNCs used in the composites were synthesized as follows: using 50 mM glutathione as a template, 125 μL of AgNO3 (20 mM) was added under magnetic stirring at room temperature and the pH was adjusted to 9.0 using sodium hydroxide. Subsequently, 5 mg of α-lipoic acid was mixed with 100 mM NaBH4 and stirred thoroughly until a clear solution was observed, at which time it was immediately added to the silver mixture and shaken and mixed. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 2 h. The resulting AgNCs were stored at 4 °C.

The synthesis of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 nanocomposites was performed using the method of Fan et al. [39] with some modifications. First, 240 μL of SMP (10 mg/mL) was added to 200 μL of AgNCs under vigorous stirring. The above complex was mixed with 1 mL of zinc nitrate hexahydrate solution (35 mM) and agitated for 5 min. Then, 1 mL of 2-methylimidazole solution (280 mM) was added and shaken to mix. The mixture was placed in the refrigerator overnight to obtain a milky white solution that was centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 10 min, after which the supernatant was washed three times. The synthesized AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 was stored at 4 °C away from light until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (20 mg/mL) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (10 mg/mL) were formulated with 1 mL of CQDs. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 30 min at room temperature to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs. Meanwhile, 1 μM amino-modified aptamer solution was heated at 95 °C in a water bath for 10 min, cooled in an ice bath for 10 min, and then left to stand at room temperature for 10 min to promote DNA folding to obtain a minimal energy structure. At the end of CQDs activation, 200 μL of denatured-reversed aptamer solution was added and mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The successfully prepared CQDs-Apt52 probe at the end of the condensation reaction was placed at 4 °C and set aside.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual fluorescent signaling proportional aptamer sensor for STR detection
ce:section.ce:para: 225 μL of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 (40 μM) was incubated with 75 μL of CQDs-Apt52 solution (1 μM) at 4 °C overnight. 50 μL of different concentrations (1 nM, 10 nM, 25 nM, 50 nM, 100 nM, 200 nM, 300 nM, 400 nM, and 500 nM) of the STR standard solution was added to the above solution and the resulting mixture was mixed and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. Then 50 μL of Cu2+ solution was added to react for 90 s and then transferred to a microcuvette. The fluorescence emission spectrums in the range of 450–800 nm were collected at the excitation wavelength of 425 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of STR in real milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh milk was purchased from local supermarkets as real samples for analysis. Pretreatment of actual samples was used to remove proteins and fats following a modified version of previous reports with few changes. Different concentration levels of STR (20 nM, 100 nM and 200 nM) were added to the milk samples before pretreatment. After that, samples were pretreated using the following method. First, 4 mL of each of the three milk samples with added targets were added to 16 mL of methanol and continuously frozen at − 20 °C for 20 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the precipitates. The supernatant was passed through a 0.22 µm filter membrane, the filtrate was dried at 60 °C and the remaining solution was dried with nitrogen. After resuspension with ultrapure water and passing through a 0.22 µm filter membrane again, the volume of the resulting solution was fixed to 4 mL. Through the above treatments, we basically removed the substrate in the milk that could affect the performance of the sensor.

The screening principle of the STR aptamer is shown in Fig. 1. The target antibiotic was mixed and incubated with the oligonucleotide library, and then MGO adsorption was used for adsorptive separation of the free ssDNA that was not bound to the antibiotic. The ssDNA bound to the antibiotic was amplified by PCR, the PCR product was eluted, and the nucleic acid concentration was determined. The obtained ssDNA was used as the next round to build a screening library. During the screening process, antibiotics such as PCN, CAP, CHP, and TET were simultaneously added to the screening system during the 6th and 10th rounds for counter-screening to enhance the specificity of the aptamers screened. The binding rates for each round are shown in Fig. S1A. As the number of selection rounds increased, the overall binding rate gradually increased, except for rounds 6 and 10, which may have been due to the successful binding of ssDNA to non-target antibiotics during these two rounds. A recovery rate of 65% was achieved on 13th round. In addition, compared with the 1st round (Fig. S1B), the gel electrophoresis of the last round in Fig. S1C showed clearer and brighter target bands in the correct positions, with no spurious bands, allowing the screening to be terminated. Therefore, the PCR products of the 13th round were cloned and sequenced.

The top-30 highly-enriched sequences from the sequencing were analyzed for their homology information and secondary structure prediction (Table S2). Based on the results, they were classified into different families and a representative sequence with highly enriched and stable secondary structure was selected in each family for binding affinity analysis. Fig. S2A shows the homology analysis of the high-throughput sequencing results using DNAMAN 8 software, and the results were roughly categorized into three large families. Fig. S2B depicts the affinity binding curves and K d of three representative candidate aptamers (Apt3, Apt5, and Apt6). K d is a measure of aptamer affinity, where a larger K d value indicates a weaker affinity of the aptamer and vice versa. Fig. S2B shows that all three candidate aptamers had a greater affinity for STR, among which Apt5 has the smallest K d value, i.e., the strongest affinity. Then, we performed truncation optimization analysis based on the secondary structures and molecular simulation of docking binding sites predicted by the Mfold program for these candidate aptamers (Fig. S2C). The truncation process is shown in Fig. S2D. The non-essential bases in the red box were sheared to obtain the five truncated aptamers Apt31, Apt51, Apt52, Apt61, and Apt62.

The binding curves of the five truncated aptamers are fitted to Fig. 2A. It was found that the K d values were lower than those before the truncation, which meant that the affinity was increased. This suggests that the deleted bases hindered the binding of the antibiotic and the aptamer. Fig. 2A shows that Apt52 has the lowest K d value of 14.52 nM. Therefore, Apt52, which had a highly specific affinity for STR, was selected for subsequent experiments to construct a ratiometric fluorescent aptamer sensor. CD characterizes conformational changes of aptamers. The CD spectrum of the target in binding buffer in Fig. 2B was close to the baseline, indicating an extremely weak CD signal. The CD spectrum of Apt52 had a positive peak at 270 nm and a negative peak at 240 nm. This indicated that Apt52 had a B-type DNA structure. The negative peak at 240 nm was significantly enhanced by the addition of the target to the aptamer, which implied a conformational change of the target embedded in the aptamer to form a stable complex. The above CD results indicated that Apt52 obtained high affinity and low base number by truncation and could be used to develop an aptasensor to achieve sensitive detection. Table S3 showed the comparison with previously reported aptamers of STR [40,41]. Zhou et al. screened the STR-specific aptamer for the first time using the magnetic bead method with a K d value of 199.1 nM. Hui et al. truncated the aptamer screened by zhou et al. in order to improve the affinity of the aptamer, and obtained S03 with a K d value of only 66.25 nM. The aptamer obtained and truncated by MGO-SELEX used in this work had a dissociation constant as low as 14.52 nM. Compared with the existing reports, the aptamer obtained by this screening method showed significantly enhanced binding to STR.

Fig. 3A shows that AgNCs and SMP-coated AgNCs encapsulated into ZIF-8 were monitored using TEM. It was found that AgNCs could present the well uniform mono-dispersion in water. Moreover, after SMP-coated AgNCs were encapsulated into ZIF-8, the yielded AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 exhibited the contoured structure of ZIF-8 profile. This is consistent with the dodecahedral structures shown in the SEM image (Fig. 3B). In addition, we investigated the elemental features of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 using EDS. As shown in Fig. 3C, the elements C, N, O, Zn and Ag were dispersed in the nanocomposites in a discrete mixture, confirming the successful synthesis of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 nanocomposites.

Fig. 3D displays the FTIR spectrum of AgNCs, ZIF-8 and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. The peak observed at 419 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of Zn-N, while the peak at 692 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of the imidazole ring. Both ZIF-8 and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 displayed vibrations of the imidazole ring between 600 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1. Typically, the CN stretch mode was credited for the prominent peaks at 1457 cm−1 and 1516 cm−1. The above results demonstrated that zinc-containing compounds with imidazole ligands interacted appropriately, indicating ZIF-8 was synthesized properly. Moreover, the N-H and C-H stretching vibrations of the imidazole ring at 2928 cm−1 and 3139 cm−1 also could be observed. However, the stretching vibrations of the characteristic peaks of CO and N-H at 1660 cm−1 and 3340 cm−1 in the AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 did not appear in ZIF-8, but came from AgNCs. These findings demonstrated AgNCs was encapsulated in ZIF-8 and that did not affect the FTIR spectrum peaks of pristine ZIF-8. The XRD patterns of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 were consistent with each other, as seen in Fig. 3E. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.4˚, 10.5˚, 12.8˚, 14.8˚, 16.5˚, 18.1˚, 24.7˚, and 26.8˚ suggested that the crystal structure of ZIF-8 was not affected by the encapsulation of AgNCs. The fluorescence intensities of equal volumes of AgNCs, SMP-coated AgNCs and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 were measured, respectively. Fig. 3F shows that the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 is 8-fold and 2-fold higher than that of AgNCs and SMP-coated AgNCs, respectively. This indicates that AgNCs were indeed encapsulated by SMP coating and ZIF-8 and gradually enhanced the fluorescence intensity.

The experimental schematic of fluorescence aptasensor is shown in Fig. 4. The aptamer-functionalized CQDs emitted green fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of 425 nm, with a fluorescence emission peak near 520 nm. At the same excitation wavelength, AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 also had a fluorescence emission peak at around 685 nm. Dual-fluorescent detection probes were prepared by relying on the porous structure of ZIF-8 to adsorb aptamer-functionalized CQDs onto the surface of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. When STR was not present, the aptamer-functionalized CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8, preventing Cu2+ from quenching fluorescence. Under 425 nm excitation, CQDs and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 displayed emission peaks near 520 nm and 685 nm, respectively. When STR was present, the aptamer bound to target underwent a conformational change, and finally detached from the surface of ZIF-8, and the response signal appeared. Cu2+ rapidly penetrated the pores of ZIF-8 and formed agglomerates with AgNCs with large particle size, and also bound to GSH to induce fluorescence quenching. The emission peak at 685 nm decreased, while the emission peak of CQDs at 520 nm remains unchanged. A ratiometric fluorescent aptamer sensor, in which I
685 /I
520 was negatively correlated with STR concentration was developed based on the above principle.

The factors affecting the response signal were examined and optimized. The results in Fig. 5A shows that AgNCs synthesized in a solution environment with pH 9.0 had the strongest fluorescence intensity. Alkaline environment increased the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs, but an excessively alkaline environment instead would decrease the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs. Therefore, pH 9.0 was optimal for synthesizing the silver nanoclusters. The results in Fig. 5B show that the fluorescence intensity of the response signaling material was sensitive to the coating solution. BSA, OVA and SMP all promoted the fluorescence intensity of silver nanoclusters, possibly because the protein coating induced energy transfer to the AgNCs thereby enhancing the red fluorescence. The strongest enhancement of the SMP coating may be due to the fact that it contains more amino acid residues, and the AgNCs-SMP interaction may induce an energy transfer of different metal-ligand residues from the SMP to the AgNCs. Therefore, SMP with the best containment effect was chosen for subsequent experiments. Notably, the concentration of the coating solution also affected the sensitivity of the response signaling material. Fig. 5C shows the effect of different concentrations of SMP on the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8. The fluorescence intensity of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 was highest when the coating concentration was 10 mg/mL. At concentrations higher than 10 mg/mL, the encapsulation of AgNCs by SMP was stronger than the enhancement of fluorescence. Hence, 10 mg/mL of SMP was chosen to coat AgNCs.

The ratio of detection probe to response material is another key factor in the effectiveness of the experiment. An excess of the detection probe will lead to an insignificant quenching response effect, while insufficient probes will reduce the detection sensitivity of the sensor. Fig. 5D shows that a volume ratio of 1:3 between the detection probe and the response material provided the best detection performance. Different concentrations of Cu2+ ranging from 1 to 800 ng/mL were introduced to a solution of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8, and the resulting fluorescence intensity after quenching was measured when containing 1 nM and 100 nM STR, respectively. Fig. 5E shows the maximum difference in fluorescence intensity with increasing Cu2+ concentration at 100 ng/mL. Therefore, 100 ng/mL of Cu2+ was chosen as the experimental concentration. Finally, the quenching time of Cu2+ was optimized to enhance the analytical performance of the sensor (Fig. 5F). The porous shell of ZIF-8 may contribute to the massive absorption and accumulation of Cu2+. Over time, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I
685 /I
520 ) gradually decreased before 90 s and remained stable thereafter, showing that a quenching time of 90 s was optimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: STR detection by proportional fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between the fluorescence signal and STR concentration was examined after optimizing the experimental conditions. As the concentration of STR increased from 1 nM to 500 nM, the peak intensity at 520 nm nearly remained constant, and the fluorescence emission at 685 nm gradually diminished ( Fig. 6A). This may be due to the fact that the addition of STR changed the state of the aptamer, which caused electron transfer between Cu2+ and AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8, resulting in the collapse of the ZIF-8 structure and fluorescence quenching at 685 nm. In addition, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 685 nm and 520 nm was negatively correlated with STR concentration (Fig. 6B). The relationship could be described by the linear regression equation y = 1.34 - 0.00221x (R 2 = 0.999). In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) of the assay was calculated to be 0.98 nM based on the principle of triple signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3). This was significantly lower than the maximum residue level of 354.56 nM for STR. The sensor prepared in this work has a lower detection limit than other STR detection methods listed in Table S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, 100 nM STR and 1000 nM other potential interfering antibiotics were used to investigate the specificity of the probe. The results showed that the probe was insensitive to non-target antibiotics, even at concentrations 10 times higher than that of STR. To test the stability, AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 and CQDs-Apt52 were stored in a closed environment in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 0–15 days. As shown in the Fig. S3, upon extending the storage time, the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm and 685 nm slightly changed, and the fluorescence signal decreased slightly by 3.56% after 15 days of storage, indicating that the two fluorescent materials have excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ability of the proportional fluorescence sensor to detect STR in milk
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of the method in real samples, milk samples were determined using the protocol developed in this paper by adding different concentrations of STR and performing testing under the optimized conditions. The recoveries of the samples were 96.4–104.6%, as shown in Table S5, indicating that the designed method can be used to detect STR.

Here, we successfully selected STR aptamer with high affinity and specificity using MGO-SELEX. Non-essential bases were removed from the original length aptamer by sequence optimization. The aptamer (Apt52), which retained only the recognition site after truncation, was highly sensitive to antibiotics, with a K d of only 14.52 nM. This ensures accurate identification of STR. A proportional fluorescent aptamer sensor for STR detection was constructed using AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 as the response signal and CQDs-Apt52 as the reference signal. The stable internal reference signals made the sensor highly resistant to interference. Fluorescence enhancement of AgNCs-SMP@ZIF-8 was achieved by protein-mediated and ZIF-8 encapsulation, allowing it to be used for sensitive fluorescence detection. The ability of the ZIF-8 porous structure to rapidly enrich for the quencher Cu2+ shortened the response time and increased the speed of STR detection. Thus, the method has a very high sensitivity with a wide linear range, low detection limit and good specificity, which provides a feasible method for the sensitive detection of other antibiotics to analyze food samples.

Wanqi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhicong Sun: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology. Yuhang Tian: Investigation, Data curation. Yaoting Mou: Investigation, Data curation. Yemin Guo: Resources. Xia Sun: Resources. Falan Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023T160393, 2022M721977), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32001781), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2020QC249), Shandong Province Science and Technology Small and Medium Enterprises Innovation Ability Enhancement Project (2022TSGC1188), Shandong Provincial Youth Innovation Team Development Plan of Colleges and Universities (2022KJ226).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135427.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wanqi Zhang: Graduate student, School of agricultural engineering and food science, Shandong University of Technology. The mean research focuses on rapid detection of antibiotics.
Zhicong Sun: Graduate student, School of agricultural engineering and food science, Shandong University of Technology. The research focuses on rapid detection of antibiotics.
Yuhang Tian: Graduate student, School of agricultural engineering and food science, Shandong University of Technology. The research focuses on rapid detection of antibiotics.
Yaoting Mou: Graduate student, School of agricultural engineering and food science, Shandong University of Technology. The research focuses on rapid detection of antibiotics.

Yemin Guo: The professor at the Shandong University of Technology, School of agricultural engineering and food science. The research focuses on safety and rapid detection technology and equipment of agricultural products.
Xia Sun: The professor at the Shandong University of Technology, School of agricultural engineering and food science. The research focuses on vegetable quality safety and quality evaluation.
Falan Li: The associate professor at the Shandong University of Technology, School of agricultural engineering and food science. The research focuses on rapid detection of antibiotics.
